title: Review planner and scheduler changes for feature coherence
description: Review the completed feature as one unit, focusing on whether the planner output contract, scheduler suppression logic, and backward-compatibility behavior remain coherent across the full change set.
acceptance_criteria: Review covers the planner template, planner prompt, scheduler detection/suppression logic, and accompanying tests together rather than as isolated fragments.
The review confirms the dependency model for shared tester/documentation/review beads is consistent with the intended scheduler behavior.
Any architectural, regression, or compatibility concerns are either resolved or explicitly handed off before approval.

handoff_summary.completed: Reviewed scheduler.py:797-890, _uses_planner_owned_followups (line 1024), _planner_owned_followup (line 1036), storage.create_bead feature_root_id propagation, and test at line 353. Traced the full execution path for the partial shared follow-up scenario.
handoff_summary.remaining: Fix scheduler.py lines 830-892: guard each of the three legacy-bead creation blocks (test_bead is None, doc_bead is None, review_bead is None) with `and not uses_planner_owned` so that planner-owned flows do not backfill missing follow-up types as per-developer children. Verify that test_scheduler_does_not_backfill_legacy_children_when_shared_followups_exist passes after the fix.
handoff_summary.risks: Suppressing legacy creation entirely for planner-owned flows means partial shared follow-up sets (e.g., planner created shared test/docs but not review) will have no review bead at all rather than a legacy fallback. The planner contract treats this as correct, but operators must be aware that all shared follow-ups must be pre-created by the planner for planner-owned features.
handoff_summary.next_action: In scheduler.py, change the three legacy bead creation conditions from `if test_bead is None:` / `if doc_bead is None:` / `if review_bead is None:` to `if test_bead is None and not uses_planner_owned:` etc. Keep the else branches (_sync_followup_scope) unconditional so planner-owned beads still get their scope synced. Then rerun review.
handoff_summary.block_reason: scheduler.py:830-890 still creates legacy per-developer docs/review children when uses_planner_owned=True but only a subset of shared follow-ups exist. The uses_planner_owned flag is correctly computed but never gates the legacy bead creation path. test_scheduler_does_not_backfill_legacy_children_when_shared_followups_exist (tests/test_orchestrator.py:353-401) is currently failing because the scheduler creates {implement.bead_id}-docs and {implement.bead_id}-review even when a shared tester bead already exists under the feature root.
handoff_summary.conflict_risks: Downstream tester/review/documentation agents may receive a mixed shared-plus-legacy topology and treat it as intentional, masking a scheduler regression in the feature-level dependency model.

guardrails.template_text:
# Review Guardrails

Primary responsibility: Inspect code, tests, docs, and acceptance criteria for correctness, completeness, and risk.

Allowed actions:
- Review only the changed files listed in the bead's touched_files and changed_files fields. Do not read unrelated files.
- Validate acceptance criteria against the implementation and handoff state.
- Block with a clear recommendation when the bead actually requires implementation work.

Efficiency constraints:
- Do not run the test suite. Testing is the tester agent's responsibility.
- Focus on correctness, completeness, and risk — not style or formatting.
- Keep the review concise. If there are no findings, say so and approve promptly.

Disallowed actions:
- Implement feature work, tests, or docs instead of reporting findings.
- Rewrite architecture or silently fix issues discovered during review.
- Mark incomplete work as accepted without evidence.

Expected outputs:
- Return JSON with `outcome` set to `completed` (reviews always complete; use `verdict` for pass/fail) and `summary` as a one-line description of the review result.
- Return JSON with structured verdict fields for every run: `verdict`, `findings_count`, and `requires_followup`.
- Treat `verdict` as the review signoff decision: `approved` means the bead can complete, while `needs_changes` means the bead must block for follow-up work.
- Use `verdict=approved`, `findings_count=0`, and `requires_followup=false` when no unresolved findings remain.
- Use `verdict=needs_changes`, set `findings_count` to the unresolved finding count, set `requires_followup=true` unless there is a stronger explicit reason not to, and always include `block_reason` when any required fix remains.
- Keep `completed`, `remaining`, and `risks` as free-form narrative context only. They inform operators, but they do not override the structured verdict or control scheduler state.
- Review findings ordered by severity, or an explicit statement that no findings were discovered.
- Clear blocked handoff details when the task belongs to another agent type.

worker_prompt_context.description: Review the completed feature as one unit, focusing on whether the planner output contract, scheduler suppression logic, and backward-compatibility behavior remain coherent across the full change set.
acceptance_criteria: Review covers the planner template, planner prompt, scheduler detection/suppression logic, and accompanying tests together rather than as isolated fragments.
The review confirms the dependency model for shared tester/documentation/review beads is consistent with the intended scheduler behavior.
Any architectural, regression, or compatibility concerns are either resolved or explicitly handed off before approval.
worker_prompt_context.conflict_risks: Downstream tester/review/documentation agents may receive a mixed shared-plus-legacy topology and treat it as intentional, masking a scheduler regression in the feature-level dependency model.

handoff_summary.completed: Reviewed the scoped planner contract and scheduler/test changes together in templates/agents/planner.md, src/codex_orchestrator/prompts.py, src/codex_orchestrator/scheduler.py, and tests/test_orchestrator.py. Confirmed the planner template and planner prompt align on shared tester/documentation/review beads for multi-bead features, and confirmed the tests cover the intended shared follow-up dependency model and review/test verdict compatibility paths.
handoff_summary.remaining: Finding 1 (high): src/codex_orchestrator/scheduler.py:804-890 still falls back to legacy per-developer docs/review creation whenever a planner-owned feature flow is missing those specific follow-ups, even though the planner contract in templates/agents/planner.md:24-27 and src/codex_orchestrator/prompts.py:147-148 requires shared follow-up beads for multi-bead features. That means a partially planner-owned setup can silently produce mixed topology instead of suppressing legacy children, which conflicts with the expectation asserted by tests/test_orchestrator.py:353-399 and undermines the acceptance criterion that planner output and scheduler suppression stay coherent as one unit.
handoff_summary.risks: If this ships as-is, feature trees that already contain one shared follow-up bead but not the full shared tester/documentation/review set can still sprout legacy per-developer follow-ups, creating inconsistent dependency graphs and weakening backward-compatibility guarantees around shared review/test/documentation scheduling.
handoff_summary.next_action: Update the scheduler's planner-owned follow-up suppression so partial shared-follow-up setups do not backfill legacy child docs/review beads, or narrow the contract/tests to match the intended fallback behavior, then rerun review.
handoff_summary.block_reason: Planner-owned shared follow-up suppression is not fully coherent with the documented planner contract: the scheduler still backfills legacy docs/review children when only a subset of shared follow-ups exists.
handoff_summary.conflict_risks: Downstream tester/review/documentation agents may receive a mixed shared-plus-legacy topology and treat it as intentional, masking a scheduler regression in the feature-level dependency model.
